author: śrīla saccidānanda bhaktibinoda ṭhākura
verses:
(mana āmāra) hu̐sā’ra theko, bhula’ nāka,
śuddha sahaja tattvadhane
noile māyāra vaśe, avaśeṣe,
kā̐date ha’be ciradine

śuddhajīve jaḍa nāi, bhāi, ṭhika bujha tāi,
nije sukhī (se) vṛndāvane
se jakhana kṛṣṇa⋅candre bhaje sukhete maje,
madhura⋅rase anukṣaṇe

jaḍadehe tāra sādhanabhakti, jñāna⋅virakti,
dehera jātrā dharmabhāve
se gṛhe thāke, vane vā thāke, majiye ḍāke,
‘kṛṣṇa’ bale’ ekamane

ekei ta’ bali sahaja⋅bhajana, śuddha mana,
kṛṣṇa pā’bāra eka upāya
ihā chāḍi’ je āropa kare, sei ta’ mare,
tāra ta’ nāhi bhajana haya

cā̐da⋅bāulera e viśvāsa, choṭa⋅haridāsa,
ekaṭu kevala vipathe cale
śacī⋅sutera kṛpāya, dūra ha’ye, hāya,
nā pāya āra gauracaraṇe
en_translation:
O my mind! Remain conscious and do not forget the wealth of pure and simple truth. Otherwise, under the sway of illusion, you will eventually have to weep eternally.

In a pure soul there is no materialism, brother; understand this clearly. The pure soul is naturally happy in Vṛndāvana. When he worships Kṛṣṇa⋅candra, he drowns in joy and relishes sweet mellows at every moment.

Within his material body he practices devotion and detachment from worldly knowledge; the journey of his body is conducted in a religious mood. Whether he lives at home or in the forest, he calls out "Kṛṣṇa!" with a single mind.

This indeed is called simple worship—with a pure mind, the one way to attain Kṛṣṇa. Whoever abandons this and imposes something artificial only perishes; he performs no true worship.

This is the belief of Cānd Bāula. Choṭa Haridāsa went but a little astray, yet by the decree of the Lord, he became distant and could no longer attain His feet.
